name: Jeff Colyer
occupation: Craniofacial/Plastic Surgeon, Former 47th Governor of Kansas
hometown: Born and raised in Hays, Kansas; currently lives in Overland Park, Kansas
education: Thomas More Prep-Marian High School (Hays, 1978); BA Economics, Georgetown University (1981); MA International Relations, Cambridge University (1982); MD, University of Kansas School of Medicine (1986)
family: Married to Ruth Gutierrez Colyer since 1991. Three daughters: Alexandra, Serena, and Dominique. Father was James Daniel Colyer, a Hays dentist for 30 years and WWII Navy veteran.
whoTheyAre:
Jeff Colyer grew up in Hays, Kansas, where his father was a dentist who served the community for 30 years. He is the only Kansas governor ever from Hays. A fifth-generation Kansan, Colyer graduated from Thomas More Prep-Marian Catholic High School in 1978 before heading east for college. He earned degrees from Georgetown University and Cambridge University in England, then came back to Kansas for medical school at KU. His mother's battle with breast cancer helped push him toward medicine.

Colyer became a board-certified craniofacial and plastic surgeon, specializing in reconstructing skull and facial deformities in children. But his most remarkable work has been overseas. Since 1985, he has volunteered as a surgeon in war zones and disaster areas through the International Medical Corps. He has operated on more than 20,000 patients in over 20 countries, including Afghanistan during the Soviet occupation, Iraq after the 2003 invasion, and Ukraine in 2024. During the 1994 Rwandan genocide, Colyer was the only surgeon in all of southern Rwanda while 800,000 people were being killed. His wife Ruth served alongside him.

Colyer married Ruth Gutierrez in 1991. She is a scientist who worked on one of the first Genetic Therapy Teams at the National Institutes of Health. They have three daughters. He was also a White House Fellow under Presidents Reagan and George H.W. Bush, and he co-authored multiple editions of The Military Balance, a leading global defense assessment, while working as an analyst in London. He now lives in Overland Park and practices surgery at Sano Orthopedics.
theirRecord:
Colyer entered Kansas politics in 2007, serving in the state House and then the Senate before becoming lieutenant governor under Sam Brownback in 2011. As lieutenant governor, he designed KanCare, the state's privatized Medicaid system, which his campaign says saved $2-2.5 billion while increasing services and covering over 400,000 patients. He also oversaw a $500 million increase in school funding over five years.

When Brownback resigned in January 2018 to become a Trump administration ambassador, Colyer became the 47th governor. His first executive order was the first mandatory sexual harassment training policy by any Kansas governor. He says he turned a $500 million deficit into an $800-900 million surplus without raising taxes, ended 50 years of school funding litigation, and achieved the state's first credit outlook upgrade in a decade. He also signed a controversial law protecting faith-based adoption agencies that refuse to place children in same-sex households.

Colyer lost the 2018 Republican primary to Kris Kobach by just 345 votes -- the narrowest defeat of an incumbent governor in a U.S. primary in history. Trump had endorsed Kobach. Kobach then lost the general election to Democrat Laura Kelly. Colyer entered the 2022 governor's race but withdrew after being diagnosed with prostate cancer, the same disease that struck his father and grandfather. He says he has made a full recovery. He carries political baggage from his seven years as Brownback's lieutenant governor during the controversial tax experiment that led to budget shortfalls, education cuts, and credit downgrades before the legislature reversed the tax cuts in 2017. Critics have said the Brownback-Colyer era "brought this state to its knees."
whatYouShouldKnow: Colyer is the only candidate in the race who has already served as governor. He managed a $17 billion state budget and 40,000 employees during his time in office.

He has volunteered as a war-zone surgeon for over 35 years through the International Medical Corps, operating on more than 20,000 patients in 20+ countries. During the 1994 Rwandan genocide, he was the only surgeon in all of southern Rwanda.
His 2025 year-end campaign filing shows $2,070,322 raised, with $1.05 million of that coming from personal loans. He had $1,884,510 cash on hand -- the most of any candidate in the governor's race.
He served as Trump's 2024 Kansas Campaign Chairman, though Trump endorsed Kobach over Colyer in the 2018 primary that Colyer lost by 345 votes.

He served seven years as lieutenant governor under Sam Brownback during the controversial tax experiment that caused severe budget problems for Kansas. Colyer says he cleaned up the finances after becoming governor.
He grew up in Hays and graduated from TMP-Marian. His father practiced dentistry in Hays for 30 years. Colyer now lives in the Kansas City suburbs.
He was diagnosed with prostate cancer in August 2021 and withdrew from the 2022 governor's race for treatment. He says he has fully recovered.

Kansas voters rejected a constitutional amendment on abortion in 2022 by a wide margin. Colyer has called abortion 'one of the civil rights issues of our time' and wants Kansas to stop being a destination for out-of-state abortion patients.
whereTheyWorship:
Jeff Colyer was raised Catholic in Hays. He attended Thomas More Prep-Marian, the Catholic high school in Hays, and the Colyer family belonged to St. Joseph's Catholic Church. His father's funeral included a rosary, a funeral Mass at St. Michael the Archangel Catholic Church in Leawood, and burial at Resurrection Catholic Cemetery in Lenexa -- all traditional Catholic rites.

Colyer's Catholic faith shows more in his actions than in public statements. He signed legislation protecting Catholic Charities and other faith-based adoption agencies, toured Catholic Charities of Wichita with his family, and has spent decades doing humanitarian surgical work in some of the most dangerous places on earth. His specific current parish has not been publicly identified, though his Catholic background is well documented.

church.name: St. Joseph's Catholic Church (family parish in Hays); current parish not publicly confirmed
church.details: The Colyer family were parishioners at St. Joseph's Catholic Church in Hays. Colyer graduated from TMP-Marian Catholic High School. His current parish in the Kansas City area has not been publicly identified.

quotes.text: I'm running for governor to make this the best place to raise a family and have a small business.
I held the line on taxes, including property taxes, and I ended 50 years of litigation over our schools that had eluded over a dozen governors.

How do we defend innocent life? I think this is one of the civil rights issues of our time. We have a Supreme Court that has found this right to abortion that is nowhere found in that constitution. It's our fastest-growing industry in the state of Kansas.

Kansas needs a proven, strong, competent conservative governor who will fight for you and who will stand shoulder to shoulder with President Trump. This is about winning. This is about taking back our state so that we are the beating heart of America once again.
Instead of handing over a $500 million deficit and $800 in the checking account of the state of Kansas, I handed Laura Kelly an $800 million surplus, and we didn't raise taxes to do that.

I will work to cut residential property taxes for everyone, and I'll also add exemptions to make sure seniors and disabled veterans can afford to stay in their homes. This starts with fully funding our schools to avoid localities having to shift the burden and increase property taxes to fill the gap.

Catholic Charities and other adoption agencies are key to the fabric of our communities. I look forward to signing this bill because it increases the opportunities for needy children to find loving homes.

campaignFinance.narrative: Colyer is the best-funded candidate in the 2026 governor's race. His year-end 2025 filing shows $2,070,322 raised with $1,884,510 cash on hand and only $185,812 spent. However, about half of that total comes from $1.05 million in personal loans he made to his own campaign -- a $50,000 initial loan in May 2025 followed by $1 million more in December 2025. That means roughly $1.02 million came from outside donors. The named donors visible in the filing are a mix of individual contributors rather than one dominant source. Multiple candidates in the 2026 governor's race have made large personal loans, and Colyer has been described alongside others as a 'millionaire candidate.'
campaignFinance.undisclosed: The full itemized donor list was not fully parsed from the state filing. The named donors above are a sample from the visible portions of the Kansas Ethics Commission report. The complete donor list is available in the official filing.
